articles.title: Brickbat: From Guard to Inmate
articles.content:
Francisco Izayas Castillo是加利福尼亚州圣克拉拉县的一名前任监狱看守，今年3月被陪审团判定犯有轻罪殴打罪，随后被判处45天监禁。该指控源于2022年发生的一起事件。当时Castillo是该监舍单元中唯一的副看守，他协助两名囚犯殴打另一名囚犯。当袭击者告诉他他们要殴打那名囚犯时，Castillo据称回应说“处理吧”，然后给他们橡胶手套，并打开了受害者的牢房门。袭击发生后，当受害者在牢房内启动紧急报警器时，Castillo将其关闭，既没有报告这起袭击，也没有通知医疗人员。他随后被解雇。

本文《砖块：从前看守到囚犯》最先发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
Francisco Izayas Castillo, a former jail guard in Santa Clara County, California, was sentenced to 45 days in jail after a jury convicted him of misdemeanor battery in March. The charge stemmed from a 2022 incident in which Castillo, the only deputy in the housing unit at the time, helped two inmates beat up a third. When the attackers told him they were going to assault the other inmate, Castillo allegedly responded, "handle it," then gave them rubber gloves and opened the victim's cell door. After the attack, when the victim activated an emergency alarm in his cell, Castillo turned it off and didn't report the assault or alert medical authorities. He was later fired.

The post Brickbat: From Guard to Inmate appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: California Public Sector Union Threatens Environmental Lawsuit Over Gavin Newsom's Return-to-Office Policy
articles.content:
加州繁琐且易于滥用的环境审查法正被工会化的州政府员工推向荒谬的新高度，他们威胁要用该法律阻止州长加文·纽森（Gavin Newsom）的返岗令。

自去年年初以来，纽森政府一直在逐步取消新冠疫情期间针对州政府员工的远程办公安排。本月早些时候，州长办公室向各州机构发出备忘录，要求他们在7月1日前实施每周四天的办公室办公要求。

州政府员工工会一直通过向州监管机构提交不公平劳动投诉、游说立法保护远程办公安排等方式，积极反对纽森的返岗政策。

据《萨克拉门托蜜蜂报》报道，这些努力在周三升级——代表州政府法律工作者的CASE工会向各机构发出信件，声称其返岗政策尚未完成《加州环境质量法》（CEQA）要求的必要审查。

CASE在信中写道，要求员工返回办公室“将需要州政府员工每月进行数十万次额外通勤，产生数十万次新的汽车出行，以及汽车尾气排放的数千吨额外空气污染”。

CASE认为，根据CEQA，各机构必须研究这些环境影响，并考虑替代方案，如继续远程办公。该工会威胁称，如果不进行CEQA审查，将提起诉讼。

州长不能在没有完成大型环境研究的情况下要求员工到办公室上班——这种说法只有那些没有关注CEQA诸多争议的人才会觉得疯狂。

简言之，该法律要求政府机构研究其开展的自主项目的环境影响，并考虑对环境影响较小的替代项目。

该法律还授权第三方起诉未进行必要CEQA审查的政府机构。

由于根据CEQA可被视为自主项目的政府行为范围广泛，且CEQA诉讼需要很长时间才能解决，该法律已成为个人和利益集团阻止他们不喜欢的任何政策或项目的首选工具。

CEQA经常被用于延迟住房、商业、基础设施等的建设。

该法律还被用于从项目发起人处获取让步。工会利用它迫使建筑商使用全工会劳动力。竞争对手开发商和社区团体利用它从项目发起人处索取现金。

著名的案例是，伯克利市的请愿者曾暂时成功阻止加州大学伯克利分校增加学生入学人数，因为该校没有研究额外学生对周边社区的噪音影响。

CEQA的荒谬结果促使加州政策制定者限制该法律的适用范围。

去年，州议会通过了一项法律，将内城住宅开发排除在需要CEQA审查的范围之外。一个由商会支持的公民投票倡议如果获得通过——可能在11月由选民审议——将大幅限制该法律对大多数基础设施和土地开发项目的要求。

然而，似乎每通过一项CEQA改革，就会出现另一个扩大该法律适用范围的新奇运用。

没有任何政策决定小到可以避免被有动机的特殊利益集团变成一份书面报告和一场法庭争斗。

CASE对纽森返岗政策的诉讼威胁，只是进一步证明CEQA已经失控的更多证据。

本文首次发表于Reason.com
articles.summary:
California's onerous and easily exploitable environmental review law is being pushed to new levels of absurdity by unionized state workers who are threatening to use it to block Gov. Gavin Newsom's return-to-office mandate. 

Since early last year, Newsom's administration has been in the process of winding down COVID-era telework arrangements for state workers. Earlier this month, the governor's office issued memos to state agencies telling them to implement a four-day, in-office requirement by July 1. 

State employee unions have actively opposed Newsom's return-to-office policies by filing unfair labor complaints with state regulators and agitating for legislative protections for telework arrangements. 

These efforts escalated Wednesday, when, as The Sacramento Bee reports, CASE, the union representing state-employed legal workers, issued letters to agencies claiming that their return-to-office policies had not undergone the necessary review required by the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA). 

Requiring workers to return to the office "will require hundreds of thousands of additional monthly commutes by state workers, creating hundreds of thousands of new car trips and thousands of tons of additional air pollution from automobile tailpipes," wrote CASE. 

Under CEQA, agencies must study those environmental impacts and consider alternatives, like continued telework, argued CASE in its letter. The union threatened to sue if this CEQA review was not performed. 

The notion that the governor can't tell his employees to physically come into the office without completing a giant environmental study will only sound crazy to people who have not been following CEQA's many controversies. 

In brief, the law requires government agencies to study the environmental impacts of discretionary projects they undertake and consider alternative projects that have lesser impacts on the environment. 

The law also empowers third parties to sue government agencies for undertaking projects without doing the necessary CEQA reviews. 

Because the range of government actions that can be considered a discretionary project under CEQA is vast, and CEQA lawsuits take a long time to resolve, the law has become a go-to tool for individuals and interest groups to block any policy or project they don't like. 

CEQA is frequently used to delay the construction of housing, businesses, infrastructure, and more. 

The law is also used to extract concessions from project sponsors. Unions use it to force builders to use all-union labor. Rival developers and community groups use it to extract cash payments from project sponsors. 

Famously, petitioners in Berkeley temporarily managed to block student enrollment growth at the University of California campus there because the school hadn't studied the noise impacts of additional students on surrounding neighborhoods. 

CEQA's absurd results have led California policymakers to limit the scope of the law. 

Last year, the Legislature passed a law that excluded infill residential development from needing to undergo CEQA review. A Chamber of Commerce-backed ballot initiative that will likely be considered by voters in November would drastically limit the law's requirements for most infrastructure and land development projects. 

Yet, it seems for every CEQA reform that's passed, there's another novel invocation of the law that expands its scope. 

No policy decision is too minor to avoid being turned into a book report and a court battle by a motivated special interest. 

CASE's threatened litigation over Newsom's return-to-office policy is just more evidence of how badly out of control CEQA has gotten.  

The post California Public Sector Union Threatens Environmental Lawsuit Over Gavin Newsom's Return-to-Office Policy appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Should You Be Allowed To Sell a Kidney? Economist Explains 'Repugnant Markets'
articles.content:
目前，数万名美国终末期肾病患者多年来一直在等待可行的移植机会，而这些机会极为稀少，且主要来自已故器官捐献者或患者的家庭成员。但诺贝尔奖得主、经济学家阿尔文·罗斯在近日接受《理性》杂志尼克·吉莱斯皮采访时表示，肾脏“实际上并不短缺”。“你有两颗，但你只需要一颗。价格机制失灵了。”

如果需要肾脏的人能够从其他人那里购买多余的肾脏，那么无尽的等待名单——及其相关的死亡——将很快消失。但他们无法做到，因为肾脏交易在美国是禁忌且被禁止的。这是罗斯所称的“令人反感的市场”的一个例子——由“令人反感的交易”构成，即“一些人想要进行的交易，而另一些显然未受伤害的人却认为不应该允许这些交易”。

当“显然未受伤害的人”得逞时，政府会进行干预以关闭市场。一旦开始寻找，就会发现令人反感的市场无处不在。赌博就是近来引发争议的一个例子：上周一，州长蒂姆·沃尔兹签署了禁止Kalshi和Polymarket等预测市场的法案。根据新法律，任何人被发现主办、宣传或协助预测市场运营都可能面临重罪指控。

支持该法律的人可能认为赌博是一种恶习，是会摧毁生命的成瘾行为，但通过禁令来压制令人反感的市场往往会产生意想不到的后果。在明尼苏达州，这可能表现为前Polymarket用户转而向不太可靠、也不太和平的庄家下注。

毒品战争是另一个更为极端的例子。罗斯说：“我非常乐意承认海洛因是不道德的。”但经过数十年的严厉定罪、扩大国家权力和侵犯公民自由，这种药物的使用仍在继续，由暴力黑市推动。“海洛因很多。过量死亡很多。社区遭到严重破坏。人类福利大量流失。”罗斯说。

禁止肾脏交易同样导致“人类福利的流失”。那么，我们如何才能恢复这种福利？

罗斯说，要使令人反感的市场合法化，不仅需要法律支持，还需要广泛的社会支持——既为了维持其法律支撑，也为了确保市场有足够的参与者运作。他说，像很多事情一样，建立共识的过程“进展缓慢”。

但这确实有先例：对某些被禁止行为的社会支持已显著增强。例如，曾经在全国范围内被禁止的同性婚姻，即使到1990年代支持率仍低于30%。如今，它在全美各地已合法化并获得认可，美国人普遍持支持态度——现在反对者不到30%，根据盖洛普的调查。

这一进程往往得到新技术的帮助：例如，堕胎药物在多布斯诉杰克逊妇女健康组织案之后，极大改善了红色州女性的处境。

尽管国家仍然限制许多自由并打压各类令人反感的市场，但在某些领域已取得显著进展。解放令人反感的市场之路漫长而艰难——但罗斯说，自由可能“在最后时刻迅速到来”。

《你应该被允许出售肾脏吗？经济学家解释“令人反感的市场”》首发于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
Right now, tens of thousands of Americans with end-stage kidney disease spend years waiting for a viable transplant, which are rare and mostly come from deceased organ donors or patients' family members. But "there's not really a shortage" of kidneys, Nobel laureate economist Alvin Roth told Reason's Nick Gillespie in a recent interview. "You have two. You only need one. There's a failure of price mechanisms."

If those in need of kidneys could buy the extra ones out of everyone else, interminable waitlists—and the associated deaths—would soon disappear. But they can't, because the kidney trade is taboo and banned in the United States. It's an example of what Roth calls a "repugnant market…which is made up of repugnant transactions," exchanges "that some people would like to engage in, and other people who aren't obviously harmed by [them] think they shouldn't be allowed to."

When those "who aren't obviously harmed" get their way, governments intervene to shut down the market.

And once you're looking for them, you can see repugnant markets everywhere. Gambling is one that has been the source of some controversy lately: Last Monday, Gov. Tim Walz (D–Minn.) signed a bill banning prediction markets like Kalshi and Polymarket in his state. Anyone caught hosting, advertising, or assisting in the operation of a prediction market could be charged with a felony under the new law.

While the law's proponents might argue that gambling is a vice and an addiction that can destroy lives, efforts to shut down repugnant markets through bans often have unintended consequences. In the case of Minnesota, that might look like a former Polymarket user placing bets with a less-than-trustworthy, less-than-entirely-pacifistic bookie instead.

The war on drugs is another, perhaps more extreme, example. "I'd love to eliminate heroin entirely," says Roth, "I'm happy to concede that heroin is immoral." But after decades of harsh criminalization, expansive state power, and civil liberties violations, use of the drug continues, enabled by a violent black market. "There's a lot of heroin. There's a lot of overdose deaths. There's lots of disruption of communities. There's lost human welfare," says Roth.

Banning the kidney trade results in "lost human welfare," too. So how can we recover that welfare?

To legitimize a repugnant market requires not only legal support, says Roth, but broad "social support" as well, both to maintain its legal backing and to ensure the market has enough participants to operate. "Like a lot of things," he says, the consensus-building process "moves slowly."

But it's certainly been done before: Social support for some forbidden practices has risen dramatically in recent years. For instance, gay marriage, once outlawed across the country, had favorability under 30 percent even into the 1990s. Today, it's legal and recognized everywhere in the U.S., and Americans are broadly in favor—now, fewer than 30 percent stand in opposition, according to Gallup.

And the process is often helped along by new technologies: Abortion pills, for instance, have done a great deal to brighten the landscape for women in red states following Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization.

While the state still limits many freedoms and cracks down on repugnant markets of all kinds, notable progress is being made in some areas. The march toward freeing repugnant markets tends to be long and grueling—but freedom can arrive "quickly at the end," says Roth.

The post Should You Be Allowed To Sell a Kidney? Economist Explains 'Repugnant Markets' appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: DHS Directs ICE To Crack Down on Allegedly Fraudulent Asylum Claims
articles.content:
国土安全部周二宣布了一份新备忘录，指示移民和海关执法局（ICE）对移民律师的文件欺诈行为实施额外处罚，包括提交虚假庇护申请。此举是特朗普政府移民打击行动的的最新举措，旨在限制移民法庭系统中的庇护案件。

声明援引国土安全部总法律顾问詹姆斯·珀西瓦尔的话，指责“数百万非法外国人”在美国移民系统中实施欺诈。珀西瓦尔继续称，“在移民法庭这种情况最为猖獗”，“代表非法外国人的移民律师几乎将每个非法外国人都说成在其祖国将遭受迫害或酷刑，这已成为标准做法。”

珀西瓦尔还声称，该备忘录授予ICE律师“更大权力执行”移民和国籍法已规定的文件欺诈处罚，并“阻止对我们庇护制度的滥用”。但国土安全部的声明没有提供ICE新政策如何实际运作的细节，该机构也没有立即回应《Reason》杂志的置评请求。

移民律师、前国土安全部庇护官维多利亚·斯latton告诉彭博法律，虽然庇护制度中确实存在欺诈，但并不像特朗普政府声称的那样普遍。斯latton继续说，“弱势案件、轻率申请和欺诈申请之间存在区别。”她表示，如果没有具体细节，很难区分弱势申请和故意欺诈申请，并担心这份模糊的备忘录可能导致一些律师不敢接手合法的庇护案件。

然而，美国移民律师协会政策与实践顾问、前庇护官希瑟·霍根告诉彭博法律，实际存在的欺诈往往源于移民祖国的有组织犯罪团伙。霍根继续说，在美国，移民律师已经接受过培训，会避免受理他们认为存在欺诈的案件。

国土安全部最近的宣布并非特朗普政府首次声称移民系统和庇护计划中存在猖獗欺诈。去年3月，特朗普总统发布了一项行政命令，称那些“指导客户隐瞒过去或在提出庇护申请时谎报情况……以试图规避移民政策”的移民律师对国家安全和国土安全构成威胁。这一措辞与特朗普第一任期时对庇护计划的言论类似，当时他也同样指责律师指导客户，并称该计划是一个“骗局”。

根据美国移民委员会的说法，庇护是一种旨在保护遭受迫害个人的移民身份类型。申请者必须已身在美国或抵达入境口岸，且必须在抵达后一年内提出申请，并符合联邦法律对难民的定义。美国移民委员会写道，申请人必须提供证据证明他们此前“在其祖国遭受过迫害……或对其未来遭受迫害有'合理恐惧'”。由于超过230万个庇护案件在3月底仍待处理，这一复杂程序平均需要四到六年才能完成。特朗普政府去年11月在一名国民警卫队成员遭枪击后决定暂停所有庇护决定，这使积压情况更加严重。但截至3月30日，除40个国家外，暂停令已被解除。

然而，能够完成这一程序的申请人一旦获得庇护，可以在美国永久居住并获得获得公民身份的途径。

一些个人无论难民身份如何都被禁止获得庇护，包括已被判犯有危险或严重罪行的外国人，或被认为对美国安全构成威胁的人。

ICE将实施什么政策来打击特朗普政府所认为的庇护计划中的猖獗欺诈，目前仍有待观察。目前尚不清楚这些政策可能如何影响已经创历史新低的庇护批准率——4月份仅为7%（相比之下，前总统乔·拜登执政期间接近50%，而特朗普第一任期时为20%至30%）。但随着特朗普继续推进每年驱逐100万人的计划，并持续大幅颠覆移民政策，数百万寻求人道主义救济者的生活悬于一线。
articles.summary:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) announced a new memo on Tuesday, directing Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to take additional steps to enforce penalties against immigration attorneys for document fraud, including filing false asylum claims. The move is the latest in the Trump administration's immigration crackdown and campaign to restrict asylum cases in the immigration court system. 

The statement quoted DHS General Counsel James Percival accusing "millions of illegal aliens" of committing fraud in the United States immigration system. "No place is this more rampant than in immigration court," Percival continued, claiming, "it is standard practice for immigration attorneys representing illegal aliens to assert that virtually every illegal alien is going to be persecuted or tortured in his or her home country." 

Percival also asserted that the memo grants ICE attorneys "greater authority to enforce" penalties for document fraud already in place under the Immigration and Nationality Act and "stop the abuse of our asylum system." But the DHS statement did not provide details on how ICE's new policies will work in practice, and the agency did not immediately respond to Reason's request for comment. 

Although fraud does exist in the asylum system, Victoria Slatton, an immigration attorney and former DHS asylum officer, told Bloomberg Law, it is not as widespread as the Trump administration claims. "There's a difference between a weak case, a frivolous claim and a fraudulent claim," Slatton continued. Without specifics, Slatton questioned how weak and intentionally fraudulent claims will be distinguished and said she fears the vague memo could cause some attorneys to fear taking on legitimate asylum cases. 

The fraud that does exist, however, tends to start from organized criminal rings in the immigrant's home countries, Heather Hogan, the policy and practice counsel at the American Immigration Lawyers Association and a former asylum officer, told Bloomberg Law. And in the U.S., immigration attorneys are already trained to avoid cases they believe are fraudulent, Hogan continued. 

The most recent DHS announcement is not the first time the Trump administration has alleged rampant fraud within the immigration system and asylum program. In March of last year, President Donald Trump issued an executive order that calls immigration attorneys who "coach clients to conceal their past or lie about their circumstances when asserting their asylum claims…in an attempt to circumvent immigration policies" threats to national and homeland security. The language resembles statements Trump made about the asylum program during his first term, when he similarly accused attorneys of coaching their clients and called the program a "scam."

Asylum is a type of immigration status meant to protect individuals from persecution, according to the American Immigration Council. To apply, foreign nationals must already be physically present in the U.S. or at a port of entry, apply within one year of their arrival, and qualify as a refugee under federal law. Applicants must provide evidence that they either previously "suffered persecution in their home country…or that they have a 'well-founded fear' of future persecution," writes the American Immigration Council. The process is complicated and averages between four and six years to complete because of the backlog of over 2.3 million asylum cases still open at the end of March. This backlog has been made worse by the Trump administration's decision to pause all asylum decisions in November following the shooting of a National Guard member. But as of March 30, the pause has been lifted for all but 40 countries. 

Applicants who are able to navigate the process, however, and are granted asylum can live in the U.S. permanently and gain a path to citizenship. 

Some individuals are barred from being granted asylum, regardless of refugee status, including foreign nationals who have been convicted of a dangerous or serious crime or who are believed to be a danger to U.S. security.

It remains to be seen what policies ICE will implement to combat what the Trump administration perceives as rampant fraud in the asylum program. It's also unclear how these policies may impact the already record-low asylum approval rating of just 7 percent in April (compared to nearly 50 percent during former President Joe Biden's administration and between 20 percent and 30 percent during Trump's first term). But as Trump moves forward with his plans to deport 1 million people a year and continues to drastically disrupt immigration policies, the lives of millions seeking humanitarian relief hang in the balance.

The post DHS Directs ICE To Crack Down on Allegedly Fraudulent Asylum Claims appeared first on Reason.com.
